coredata.dc:title: Composition analysis of Magnolia flower and their use for highly bright carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Qiulian
Li, Weihua
Qiao, Xiaolong
Zhao, Haiguang
coredata.dc:description: Colloidal carbon dots (C-dots) have excellent optical properties and they have been widely used for various types of applications. Compared to the C-dots prepared using chemicals, the Biomass-derived C-dots have attracted a great of attention because of their environmental friendliness and biocompatibility. However, currently obtained C-dots using biomass have low quantum yield. In this work, we demonstrated an efficient precursor of Magnolia flower for synthesizing high-quantum yield C-dots via hydrothermal approach. High performance liquid chromatography and Bradford assay were used to analyze the compositions of the Magnolia flower, which determine the quality of the C-dots. The typical emission bands of the as-prepared C-dots cover from 400 to 550 nm at different excitation wavelengths. The C-dots synthesized using violet Magnolia flower have the QY as high as 11.9%, which is higher than that of 10.4% (white Magnolia flower), because the violet color gives the higher content of the N. The as-obtained C-dots have excitation-dependent color and high quantum yield, showing a great potential for the use of the anti-counterfeiting system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnolia Flower
Carbon Dots (C-dots)
High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of the Magnolia flower juice
Component analysis of Magnolia flower juice

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The detailed preparation process of the C-dots.

(a) TEM image of ML-C-dots. (b) TEM image of MD-C-dots. Insets of (a)-(b) are the HRTEM images of individual C-dot and size distribution calculated based on the TEM images. (c) FT-IR spectroscopy of the ML-C-dots and MD-C-dots.

(a) The full survey XPS spectra of the ML-C-dots. (b) The full survey XPS spectra of the MD-C-dots. (c, e) The high-resolution XPS spectra of the C l s, N 1 s of the ML-C-dots. (d, f) The high-resolution XPS spectra of the C l s, N 1 s of the MD-C-dots.
PL spectra of the ML-C-dots (a) and MD-C-dots (b) upon excitation at different wavelengths. (c) Fluorescence decay curves of the ML-C-dots and MD-C-dots. (d) QY of the ML-C-dots and MD-C-dots.

The designed and printed fluorescent patterns on cotton fabric with ML-C-dots ink under room light and illumination of 365 nm and 395 nm. (a) was the designed pattern, (b) was the photo of the non-fluorescent paper under room light, (c) and (b) showed the fluorescent patterns under the illumination of 365 nm and 395 nm, respectively.
Monosaccharide and disaccharide content in MLJ and MDJ.
Polysaccharide content in MLJ and MDJ.
Protein and nitrogen content in MLJ and MDJ.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: K was the conversion factor of nitrogen content.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Lifetimes and QYs of the C-dots.

head.ce:title: Composition analysis of Magnolia flower and their use for highly bright carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Textiles & Clothing, State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles, Qingdao University, No. 308 Ningxia Road, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Textiles & Clothing, State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Textiles & Clothing, State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles, Qingdao University, No. 308 Ningxia Road, Qingdao 266071, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Colloidal carbon dots (C-dots) have excellent optical properties and they have been widely used for various types of applications. Compared to the C-dots prepared using chemicals, the Biomass-derived C-dots have attracted a great of attention because of their environmental friendliness and biocompatibility. However, currently obtained C-dots using biomass have low quantum yield. In this work, we demonstrated an efficient precursor of Magnolia flower for synthesizing high-quantum yield C-dots via hydrothermal approach. High performance liquid chromatography and Bradford assay were used to analyze the compositions of the Magnolia flower, which determine the quality of the C-dots. The typical emission bands of the as-prepared C-dots cover from 400 to 550 nm at different excitation wavelengths. The C-dots synthesized using violet Magnolia flower have the QY as high as 11.9%, which is higher than that of 10.4% (white Magnolia flower), because the violet color gives the higher content of the N. The as-obtained C-dots have excitation-dependent color and high quantum yield, showing a great potential for the use of the anti-counterfeiting system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Colloidal carbon dots (C-dots) are fluorescent nanomaterials, showing a composition tunable optical property. The C-dots consist of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, without toxic elements compared to inorganic quantum dots. Benefiting from their excellent optical properties, low biotoxicity, good biocompatibility, tunable photoluminescence and easy functionalization, C-dots have made remarkable progress in the fields of drug delivery (Kariminia et al., 2024; Consoli et al., 2023; Shu et al., 2017; Carbon Nanodots, 2024), bioimaging (Shu et al., 2017; Qin et al., 2019), optical sensing (Keçili et al., 2023; Hu et al., 2020a), luminescent solar concentrator (Li et al., 2021, 2023; Meng et al., 2023), light emitting diodes (Hu et al., 2023; A Bright and Stable, 2024; Engineering Rhombus, 2024), photocatalysis (Yuan et al., 2023; Hu et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2024; Photocatalytic; Wang et al., 2021) and fluorescence anti-counterfeiting (Carbon Dots, 2024; Zhao et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023) and so on (Hu et al., 2020b; Jiang et al., 2018; Sul et al., 2023; Ngo et al., 2023).

C-dots can be produced via a simple and ambient conditions using various types of precursors. Typically, compared to commercial chemicals, the biomass is widely used as low-lost and abundant precursors for the synthesis of C-dots. According to the definition of the International Energy Agency (IEA), biomass contains carbon absorbed by plants through photosynthesis. When this biomass is used to produce energy, the carbon is released during combustion and simply returns to the atmosphere, making modern biomass a promising near zero-emission material which has realized high value resource utilization in many fields (Shen et al., 2024; Xu et al., 2022; Zhang, 2020; Qiao et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2019).

To date, although synthetic chemicals and various types of biomasses have been selected and used for C-dots production, the search for low-cost and environmentally-friendly C-dots synthetic materials remains a challenge. Typically, biomass is rich in carbohydrates, protein, lignin and so on, making it a good source of C-dots synthesis (Tang et al., 2022). Converting biomass into C-dots is one of the effective ways to reduce resource waste and achieve sustainable development. At present, there have been many studies on the synthesis of C-dots derived from wood (Jiang et al., 2018; Shi et al., 2019), shrimp and crab shells (Bu et al., 2019; Shekarbeygi et al., 2020), fruit skins (Sul et al., 2023; Tang et al., 2022; Olmos-Moya et al., 2022), flowers (Bu et al., 2019; Shekarbeygi et al., 2020) and leaves (Hu et al., 2020b; Krishnaiah et al., 2022; Shan et al., 2022). Unfortunately, the currently obtained fluorescent quantum yield (QY) of these C-dots is lower than 10%, and the luminescence falls mostly in the blue region (less than 450 nm). Therefore, in the preparation process of biomass derived C-dots, most researchers have chosen some synthetic chemical reagents containing N, S and other elements as additives as dopants to improve the luminescence performance and the QY of C-dots. For example, Krishnaiah et al. (2022) used Kentucky bluegrass and ethylenediamine to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon dots, QY is only 7%.

Understanding the chemical composition of biomass precursors can reduce or avoid the abuse of chemical reagents, make full use of its active components, and achieve the preparation of biomass C-dots with high conversion and high yield. At present, the carbohydrate analysis of biomass mainly uses phenol-sulfuric acid spectrophotometry to determine the total carbohydrate content, ion chromatography and gel chromatography to determine the monosaccharide, disaccharide and polysaccharide content, and Bradford assay to determine the protein. These methods need to extract, separate and purify carbohydrate components, and the steps are complicated and time-consuming.

Magnolia is native to China, with beautiful flowers and pleasant aroma. It is a kind of flowers with high ornamental value and is widely distributed in various regions of China. In traditional Chinese medicine, Magnolia is also called Xinyi, which can be used as medicine and has certain edible and medicinal value. In this study, Magnolia flower was selected as resource for the production of the high-quality C-dots. The carbohydrates of the Magnolia juice were accurately analyzed by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and the protein was detected by Bradford assay. Furthermore, colloidal C-dots were prepared by hydrothermal method using white or violet Magnolia juice. The as-prepared C-dots have the absorption ranging from 200 to 400 nm with an emission peak centered at 400 and 350 nm for Magnolia liliiflora derived C-dots, and Magnolia denudata derived C-dots, respectively. The C-dots have the QY as high as 11.9%, 10.4%, respectively. Only the soluble compounds in the flower juice were used as the C-dots synthesized from natural carbon and heteroatom sources, and the fluorescence intensity and QY were still good, which showed the great superiority and broad prospect of the synthesis of self-doped C-dots in Magnolia. As a proof-of-concept, the as-prepared C-dots were used as fluorescent materials for anti-counterfeiting code, showing a high level of security.

Violet flowers of Magnolia liliiflora (ML) and white flowers of Magnolia denudata (MD) were harvested from the campus of Qingdao University. Sulfuric acid, crystalline bovine serum protein (BSA), Coomassius bright blue G250 staining solution, Phosphate Buffer solution (PBS, PH7.0) purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. All chemicals were used as received and no further purification was required. Glucose, xylose, arabinose and cellobiose were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co., Ltd., and purified by chromatography.

Before the experiment, the collected Magnolia petals were washed with distilled water repeatedly. Clean petals of ML and MD were ground separately in a mortar, and then the flower muds were filtered with a filter cloth to obtain the flower juice used for hydrothermal reaction experiments. Among them, the flower juice of Magnolia lilifloar is named as MLJ, Magnolia denudata flower juice named as MDJ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the Magnolia flower juice
ce:section.ce:para: According to the standard procedure, the moisture and inorganic content of the flower juice was analyzed (Table S1), then the carbohydrate and protein content was determined and calculated according to National Renewable Energy Laboratory.

Determination of monosaccharide and disaccharide content: the monosaccharides and disaccharide in flower juice were determined by HPLC. First, the PH of the flower juice was 5–6, and centrifuged (15,000 r/min, 20 min), and the supernatant was filtered for testing. HPLC conditions: chromatographic column Bio. Rad Aminex HPX-87 H, eluent 0.002 mol/L H2SO4, flow rate 0.6 ML/min, column temperature 50 ℃, sample amount 10 μL, differential refraction detector (RID, 45 ℃). The corresponding content of the sample was calculated according to the fitting curve (Figure S1) of the standard sample.

Determination of polysaccharides: flower juice of the two colors Magnolia was added with H2SO4 to 4 wt%, and then hydrolyzed (121 ℃, 1 h). The hydrolyzed juice was centrifuged, and the supernatant was filtered (filtration film pore size 0.22 μm), and then tested by HPLC under the same conditions as above. The concentration of polysaccharides in flower juice was calculated according to the measured monosaccharide content after hydrolysis. The measured glucose concentration was multiplied by a coefficient (0.9) to obtain the concentration of glucan, and the concentration of xylose and arabinose was multiplied by a coefficient (0.88) to obtain the concentration of xylan and araban, respectively. The corresponding content of the sample was calculated according to the fitting curve (Figure S1) of the standard sample.

Determination of protein content: the protein in flower juice was determined by Bradford assay. First, for making standard curve, took 7 test tubes and performed each concentration triply according to Table S2. After added the standard protein solution and Coomassius bright blue G250 staining solution, shook tube immediately, used the first group as the blank control within 5–20 minutes, measured the light absorption value at wavelength 595 nm, and filled in Table S2. Then, the standard protein curve (Figure S2) was drawn with the standard protein concentration as the horizontal coordinate and the absorbance at 595 nm as the vertical coordinate. Next, took 1.0 g of Magnolia petals to be tested, ground and juicing them with 10 ML PBS, and centrifuged them in two steps (12,000g, 6 min and 26,900g, 16 min) after juicing, the supernatant was obtained as the protein extract. Mixed 0.1 ML of protein extract with 5 ML of Coomassius bright blue G250 staining solution, the absorbance could be determined after 2 min. Each sample was repeated 5 times, and the average absorbance was substituted into the standard curve equation to find the protein concentration of the sample. Finally, the protein content of Magnolia could be obtained by substituting formula.

MLJ and MDJ were respectively placed in a hydrothermal reactor and reacted in an oven at 200 ℃ for 12 h. Finally, the solutions of two different Magnolia C-dots were dialyzed for 24 h and refrigerated at 4 ℃. The detailed preparation process of the C-dots was schematically shown in Fig. 1.

The morphologies and structures of the as-prepared C-dots were measured by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL 2100 F, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet 6700, USA) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, AXIS Supra, Japan) were used to characterize the surface chemical structure of C-dots. The absorption property of the C-dots was characterized by a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Japan); the fluorescence spectrum, fluorescence lifetime and QY of the purified C-dots were characterized by steady state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000, Britain), assisted with an integration sphere. Zeta potential was measured on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZSE (DLS-Zeta potential, Britain).

The as-obtained ML-C-dots solution was filtered by a 0.45 μm filter and further filtered by a 0.22 μm filter, and the obtained clear mixture was directly used for the pattern printing. The fluorescent ink was printed on non-fluorescent paper by an inkjet printing machine (model: Canon TS3480).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Component analysis of Magnolia flower juice
ce:section.ce:para:
The C-dots were prepared via a hydrothermal reaction by dispersing the as-obtained Magnolia juice as showed in Fig. 1. To understand the compositions effect of the Magnolia juice on the properties of the C-dots, we measured the water and ash content of Magnolia petals firstly, and the results were showed in Table S1. In both types of flowers (white color or violet color), carbohydrates, including glucose, xylose, arabinose, cellobiose, and some of their oligosaccharides, were abundant. The content of monosaccharides and disaccharides in flower juice of the ML and MD was both higher than that of polysaccharides. The results also showed higher total carbohydrate content in violet flower juice than that in white flower, which might be due to the synthesis of pigments requires more energy. The content of monosaccharides, disaccharides and polysaccharides in MLJ and MDJ was shown at
Tables 1 and 2, respectively. The overall carbohydrate content was 33.017 g/L, 15.899 g/L for MLJ and MDJ, respectively. The R2 of each fitting curve (Figure S1) was controlled above 0.999, which meant that it was accurate to determine the carbohydrate content of the Magnolia flower juice by HPLC, and it was rich in carbohydrates.

Amino acid is the basic unit of protein, and amino acid is the organic nitrogen compound formed by inorganic nitrogen assimilation. In the study, protein content was identified in both types of flower juice. The nitrogen content calculated based on protein content in violet flowers was 1.33 times than that of white flowers ( Table 3).

Magnolia also contains essential oils (Citral, Syringoleic acid, Trans-α-Farnesene, δ-Cadinene and etc.), volatile components (1, 8-cineole, Farnesol, Sabinene, β-pinene, α-pinne, Camphor and others.), magnoflorine, alkaloids, magnolin, capric acid, rutin, oleic acid, vitamin A and other medicinal ingredients (Kato et al., 2017; Joo et al., 2020; Diversity of Essential, 2024). These components could provide a good carbon source for the synthesis of C-dots and a good nitrogen source for the element doping of C-dots.

The morphologies and sizes of the as-prepared C-dots were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1a-b, the ML-C-dots exhibited typically spherical shape with an average particle size of 3.9 ± 0.2 nm. The measured d-spacing is around 0.32 nm, corresponding to (002) of graphite (Carbon Nanodots, 2024; Tang et al., 2022). The MD-C-dots exhibited typically spherical shape with an average particle size of 10.1 ± 0.1 nm. The measured d-spacing is around 0.28 nm, matching well with that of the graphite carbon (Carbon Nanodots, 2024). The clear lattice also indicated the good crystalline structure in C-dots benefited from high temperature carbonization (200 ℃).

The surface chemical composition of the ML-C-dots and MD-C-dots were analyzed by XPS, and the results were shown in
Fig. 3a, b and Table S3. Based on the full survey of XPS results, the as-prepared ML-C-dots consisted of C, N, and O elements, with atomic percentages of 47.10%, 6.05%, and 21.95%, respectively. The spectrum of the C 1 s could be demonstrated into five bonds centered at 284.80 eV, 286.09 eV and 287.86 eV, assigned to C−C/CC, C−O/C−N and CO bands (Fig. 3c) (Tang et al., 2022; Fu et al., 2022). The spectrum of the N 1 s could be demonstrated into three bonds located at 398.04 eV, 399.65 eV and 401.56 eV, assigned to CN−O, N-H and C-N-C bands (Fig. 3e) (Wang et al., 2020).

For MD-C-dots, it was also consisted of C, N, and O elements, with atomic percentages of 47.78%, 2.39%, and 20.21%, respectively (Fig. 3b). The spectrum of the C 1 s could be demonstrated into three bonds located at 284.80 eV, 286.21 eV and 287.96 eV, assigned to C−C/CC, C−O/C−N and CO bands (Fig. 3d) (Fu et al., 2022). The spectrum of the N 1 s could be demonstrated into three bonds located at 398.45 eV, 399.74 eV and 401.76 eV, assigned to CN−O, N-H and C-N-C bands (Fig. 3f) (Wang et al., 2020).

Consistently, the FT-IR results indicated that both the ML-C-dots and ML-C-dots had chemical bonds of CO/CC, C-C/C-H, C-N, and C-O-C (Tang et al., 2022; Senol and Bozkurt, 2020). According to the XPS and FT-IR analysis results, it could be concluded that the surface of C-dots contains -NH2, -OH and CO, which enabled the good solubility of the C-dots in aqueous solution (Fig. 2c).

The as-produced C-dots had a typical absorption ranging from 200 to 400 nm, made them colorless. Their characteristic peaks were around 240–280 and 300–350 nm, which might be induced by π-π* transition of CC and n-π* transition of N-H and CO, respectively (Zhao et al., 2022; Bu et al., 2019).

The as-prepared ML-C-dots and MD-C-dots had the excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra with excitation wavelengths in the range of 260–420 nm (Fig. 3a-b). The typical emission bands of the ML-C-dots covered from 400 to 550 nm at different excitation wavelengths, and the those of the MD-C-dots covered from 400 to 500 nm. We found out a significant PL peak variation between ML-C-dots and MD-C-dots. On the identical synthetic conditions and measurement conditions, the ML-C-dots showed a strong red-shift of its emission peak compared to that of the MD-C-dots. Considering that the composition analysis indicated that the ML have superior N content to MD, we deduced that the nitrogen doping explains the longer emission peak of the ML-C-dots compared to MD-C-dots. The fluorescent C-dots under different lights were showed in Figure S3. We further measured the QY of the C-dots using integration sphere. The ML-C-dots have a QY of 11.9%, which is higher than that of the MD-C-dots (10.4%), consistently with their lifetime values (Fig. 3c, d, e and Table 4). In addition, the obtained QY value (11.9%) was among the best in the past studies for the C-dots derived from biomass. For instance, Fu et al. synthesized self-doped C-dots from animal bones with QY up to 8.4% (Fu et al., 2022), and Sharma et al. synthesized C-dots with QY of 9.6% by rose petals (Pansari et al., 2023), even though they added some extra chemicals to achieve element doping.
Zeta potential curves indicated that the surfaces of ML-C-dots and MD-C-dots were negatively charged and had good stability (Figure S4).

As the ML-C-dots had a higher QY and wider emission bands compared to the MD-C-dots, we used the ML-C-dots solution for fluorescent patterns preparation. We used Fig. 5a as a designed pattern to print the fluorescent pattern, which could be clearly seen that there was no any visible pattern in the paper at room light (Fig. 5b), but a clear fluorescent patterns could be seen under the illumination of 365 nm (Fig. 5c) and 395 nm (Fig. 5d). In addition, the patterns had blue color under 365 nm illumination and green color under 395 nm illumination, because of the excitation dependent PL behavior of the ML-C-dots. After switching off the excitation, the fluorescent patterns completely disappeared.
The above results indicated that we could fabricate a flexible fluorescent code for multiple-level anti-counterfeiting.

As a summary, we demonstrated Magnolia juice derived C-dots for high-level security codes. We quantitatively analyzed the dominant composition of the Magnolia juice using HPLC and we found that the Magnolia juice consisted of monosaccharides, disaccharide and polysaccharide, among them, the concentration of Magnolia liliiflora juice was 33.017 g/L, the concentration of Magnolia denudata juice was 15.899 g/L. We also used Bradford assay to detect protein in the flower juice, and its concentration was 0.196 g/L, 0.148 g/L, corresponding nitrogen content was 0.032 g/L, 0.024 g/L, respectively. The results showed that Magnolia juice could be used as a carbon and nitrogen source for C-dots synthesis. The as-prepared C-dots had the emission bands covering from 400 to 550 nm with absorption ranging from 200 to 400 nm, with the QY as high as 11.9%. The higher nitrogen content leads to the red-shift of the PL peak, indicating that we can control the optical properties of the C-dots by selecting the color of the Magnolia flower. The as-prepared C-dots exhibited a strong excitation-dependent PL behavior, made them promising candidates for security code preparation. As a proof-of-concept, we designed and prepared a security code with high security level.

Weihua Li: Software. Xiaolong Qiao: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Data curation. Haiguang Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Qiulian Liu: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: H. Zhao thanks the funding supports from the Youth Innovation Team Project of Shandong Provincial Education Department.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.indcrop.2024.118416.
